speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is George Gottlieb. Today is September 24, 1996, Tape 5. Mr. Gottlieb, we were talking about Braunschweig. You were beginning to say about the Allied forces were coming in.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Allied forces bombed. Basically, in three days there's no more factory left.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the-- of the inmates, the prisoners?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let's call it the first time that we-- we called it that Red Cross packages were falling from the sky. I'm not sure they were bombs. They were not Red Cross packages. But we-- we had the feeling that we are coming towards-- towards Allied victory. We are coming to the victory. We are coming to the end of the suffering, although by already this time, which was sometime the end of March of 1945, by this time I was weak. I was-- my brother was a lot less stronger and in a bad shape. But somehow we had a feeling that we have a-- we have a hope. We have a chance. We have-- we had something to look forwards. Before we heard sometimes some kind of gunshots, but very little. But these three days was the confirmation of strength, the confirmation of who is really the master. First day, they started us to put us back to work and tried to pull out some equipment and this one. Then the bombs came again at the end of that second day and the third day in the morning. Finally, they emptied the camp and we-- they put us on the road marching. This marching was the most horrendous thing, because we marched day and-- practically day and night why the-- while our guards had the motorcycles, the cars, the trucks. And we were on the road just marching. And the-- the march was like a death march. It was really a death march, because anybody who get at the end of the line, forget about not being able to cope-- cope or get-- lie down or not to go. I mean, that was machine gun, automatic. But even the end of the line sometimes they just pushed a couple of machine guns that they should move faster. I mean, it's easier for them. They were bicycles and motorcycles and cars and trucks. And they had-- they were-- they had the physical strength to do it. We didn't. And If it wouldn't have been from-- for the support and help and for the more than support of my brother, physical support of my brother, I don't thinks I would have made it. By that time already, I was weak and sick. And finally we arrived to a camp called Ravensbruck, R-A-V-E-N-S-- V-E-N-S-B-R-U-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you are still in the country of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Germany. It was still in Germany, some-- somewhere near-- near the northern part of-- of Germany. Ravensbruck, we probably stayed there a week doing absolutely nothing. I mean, maybe the only advantage was that at least we had somewhere shelter over our head into a barrack or somewhere where we were able to-- to sleep. But back to the routine of the appeal and counting and god knows how-- how-- how small and how wonderful that that so-called shelter was. But at least there was something there. And from there, within a short while, probably a week after, they started to march us again. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we marched. And we arrived to a place called Neubrandenburg, N-E-A-- yeah. N-E-A-U-B-R-U-D-E-N-B-R-G. I had to think about. That's also in Germany, by the way. And this-- this is also the northern part of Germany, where Neubrandenburg, there was some kind of-- some kind of barracks. But I really honestly don't even remember it. Neubrandenburg, my memory is already extremely vague. I had-- had a stomach typhoid, dysentery, and really the end of my life. The only thing what I remember is a pile, but pile and pile of bodies. I mean bodies were stacked up like-- like three this way and three this way, I don't know, six, seven, eight high, as long as they could throw them up.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of a camp-- what kind of a camp was Neubrandenburg?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a camp. I really-- don't ask me. I don't know. I really, really honestly-- memory was I don't believe that was-- maybe it was a-- maybe it was a labor camp. Maybe it was just a concen-- it was a camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I could just back up for one second, when you went march-- your death march from Braunschweig to Ravensbruck, about how long did that take in walking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two weeks, roughly, in the area of 10 days, 2 weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Nourishment? What kind of nourishment?

speaker: interviewee
text: Same thing. Breakfasts, you got a cup of coffee. Sometimes you didn't. Sometimes you did. Sometimes you slept on the fields. Sometimes you slept in-- in a barn. I mean, there was no-- no place where there was-- no, no rhyme or reason or connections where we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How much of that time was in actual marching? How much of--

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day. Every day, sometimes even through the night. They-- they just-- they couldn't care less about what's happening. And from there, Neubrandenburg--

speaker: interviewer
text: To Ravensbruck was how long, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Three days, probably. I think so. It was around three days. By that time, I think that my memory is-- it's really and honestly, I don't think so that it should be-- it should be something even said, because it was-- I was in-- mentally in such a-- mentally, physically in such a condition that I didn't know if I'm-- I'm alive or-- dead or alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in Neubrandenburg for how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there approximately a week, maybe a week and a half. I don't know. That's-- finally we-- I lied just down in the courtyard, when one morning, although we heard some shooting, but very little shooting around us, suddenly the SS guards started to assemble all those who were able to stand and walk. And-- and I thought that they are going to be taken away on a new march. And I just-- I wasn't in a position to even-- even to get on my knees, never mind about getting up and marching. Then the SS guard took all those who were standing in the middle of that courtyard and machine-gunned them down and killed them, every one of them. And those who were standing and being machine-gunned down included my own brothers, right in front of us, right in front of me. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And you had no more-- I had no more desire even to-- I mean, to breathe. Forget about anything else, because anything else I couldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: The SS guard left and within half an hour-- I don't think so, it was more than a half an hour. Suddenly tanks rolled in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tanks rolled in and the first time that I have seen a white star on a tank. No idea. The first reaction was, did the Russians ran out of red paint? I mean, we didn't know what that insignia meant.

speaker: interviewee
text: And not one, but many tanks, trucks came in. And the hatches opened up and the people coming out of the tanks, they were black. I would say that 70%, 75% of that force was black. And the first time in my lifetime, saw a black human being physically. I mean, I've seen them in movies, American movies as a kid. But I never saw a black person whose skin was black.

speaker: interviewer
text: So automatically, the first-- that we knew that they are black, they are Americans. They couldn't be French. They couldn't be English. They couldn't be Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these troops were-- well, what I know now, I didn't know it then, that this was the second camp what they liberated in two days. So the previous day, they liberated another camp. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you, the liberation? What was it like for you? What feelings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Think about it, that here are guys taking off their clothes, their shoes, handing out packages, handing out cans, handing out whatever they had, trying to do their other human. And if they would have known that any one of them who were unlucky enough by the goodness of these people that they had a sandwich, that sandwich killed that person whom they liberated. The body could not take the calories. I was lying on top of three packs of what they call k-rations. K-rations basically are-- even I understand exist American army, the box which has 6,000, 7,000 calorie of food in it, which is wrapped in a brown paper. I know it today by heart, with wax all around it, that in case if it falls into the water, even that the American soldier is able to open it. I didn't have the strength to do anything else but lie-- but really lay on top of it. And that's the reason why I'm alive. These guys left within 10 minutes after. I know that I had a pair of shoe and a pair of pants from one of the guys. Suddenly the next troop came in. And the next troop looked to me like men from the Mars, or men from somewhere outer space. We realized that they are Americans because they are wearing a similar uniform and they had a armband with the Red Cross on it. Now, these guys had rubber boots all the way around, you know, like a fisherman has it all the way to the hip, and rubber gloves all the way to the shoulder and masks and God knows what. And suddenly came with a pump and they started to spray something on us. My thinking was at that time that these guys had-- these guys have seen the movie, but they don't know what real life is, because I don't need baby powder. But within seconds, I realized what they did. We didn't know what it was, but within seconds, I had no more lice. Before that, we were infected by millions of those lice. Later on found out what they sprayed, it was DDT. I mean, thank goodness today's world doesn't use DDT. But DDT in seconds killed all the lice. I was taken by these Red Cross people on a stretcher. I was put on a stretcher and was taken into a school building right there, which became some kind of a hospital or whatever you want to call it, the Red Cross shelter.

speaker: interviewer
text: There in--

speaker: interviewee
text: In Neubrandenburg. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there how long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh my goodness. A long, long time. And I would like to tell you that according to records, I was 32 kilos. 32 kilos is roughly 70 pounds. Sometimes in June, July, they taught me how to walk, how to stand up and walk, because I couldn't. They had a tough time to find even the skin available, injections. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I know The vein they had no problem to find, but they couldn't find anything in the back or anything in the arm or skin sometimes for days. And they started to bring called [INAUDIBLE]. I stayed there sometimes in September, October. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the only hope or only desire I had-- and that was a Hungarians, all kinds of nationals is there. It was a United Nations, basically, Russians, Romanian, Polish Jews, Lithuanian Jews who were liberated and who are in this camp little by little getting back to health and little by little going wherever they could go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: And we decided, five of us Hungarian boys, that we are going back to Hungary. And my only aim and-- how would I say it?-- desire to do something is to find out what happened. And I was convinced in my heart that Mommy's alive and I have to be there. It took us a long, long time to-- to ride on Russian trains and you're go over from one to another, and finally through Prague, where we went to the Jewish center where they helped us. They gave us excellent food in the Jewish center, the Jewish synagogue in Prague. It was perfect. We slept in the-- in the synagogue on the floor. And finally, we arrived to Hungary. And Hungary, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after-- after arriving the Turks-- the second day I went to our house, where it used to be-- the house was totally demolished and blown up-- and found a couple of the neighbors. I found the famous driver, you know, who drove me home from Budapest. And he told me that there is nobody from my family has ever come to there. He was very helpful and asked me, as a matter of fact, to stay with him, although I found one Jewish family who survived, a-- a man, wife, and a daughter. Their son, who was a close, close friend, did not survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I found out-- the next day I found out that-- from my auntie who survived, who lived in another town, and found out that Mom was killed in Bergen-Belsen. Bergen-Belsen, they were together. And sometimes the end of March, Mom was killed. Mom died. And basically, that settled my desire-- or forget about desire. That settled my-- my time in Hungary. I had no rhyme and reason to be there and I had no the-- no disposition whatsoever to do anything else but dislike the people who kicked me out, who took away-- I still say it today, they raped me of my-- of youth, of my-- my whole life, of love, of parents, of brother, of family.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do I do there? I have no rhyme or reason. So a cousin who survived, the two of us, we decided that we are going to Germany. And where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we are going to go to DP camp if we have to. And from there, we'll see what's going to happen to our life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to a camp, Leipheim. Leipheim is near Ulm, U-L-M. Leipheim is L-E-I-P-H-E-I-M. And in Leipheim, basically we spent-- spent the time at the beginning with not too much, but not too much doing, not too much what I would call it constructive work. Then suddenly children started to come in. I mean, I'm talking about I was a child, but I was already a big man. I was 17. So you're 17, you're already a-- more than a child. But little-- from one to five-year-old little orphans started to come in. And they started to organize whoever was volunteering, whoever was willing to do, to take care of these children, to try to bring them up, to try to teach them, to try to change the diapers, if you had to. It's-- you learned.

speaker: interviewer
text: These children were-- were--

speaker: interviewee
text: All orphans, all Jewish kids coming out of Hungary, Czechoslovakia, from any Russian-occupied, behind the Iron-- what you call here behind the Iron Curtain. We didn't know what does it mean at that time, and I don't care. And little by little, we get organized. Organized, I mean that's Jewish agency, but mostly UNRRA, you know, U-N-R-R-A, I don't know what it stands for. United-- not-- didn't mean United Nations. There was no United Nations at that time. Whatever it was. And an interesting organization by the name of ORT. And I don't have to tell you what ORT is. But what ORT had to do with it, I don't know. But ORT gave us tremendous help.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: From physical to financial to food to-- to try to explain to us what to do, what not to do and--

speaker: interviewer
text: What to do and what not to do in regard to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Regard to what to do-- how to teach a child, what to do with a child. We had no experience. I mean, I was a child myself who also-- I received love, the tender loving care from my parents. But I never saw how a diaper is put on or not to put on. But--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they get financial assistance?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. Definitely we got financial assistance of trying to buy whatever we could buy in Germany from the Germans, diapers or-- what we couldn't, we-- we-- we organized through the American Army, especially the American Army had the American Army stores. And through the people from ORT and through-- through some American soldiers or through the UNRRA, we were able to buy stuff at the American Army store, which was outstanding not only value-wise, but we had to have the money to do it. And that's the way we get it through ORT and through UNRRA, tremendous help.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did ORT give you anything specific?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a certificate even today from ORT which shows that I'm a teacher and-- teacher of what, I don't know. But anyway, they classified me with a picture, with a colored picture. And we had American uniforms with all kind of false decoration on the arm that we should look similar to an American soldier. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would this help you enable you to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it helped us to cross the borders, because we were afraid of nothing and nobody. Basically nobody could do anything else, but what we could do is try to bring these little orphans on, try to give them some kind of comfort, what at-- what at that country or whatever they were. There was just nobody there and they were in a horrible, horrible mental, physical, or no parents, no nothing, I mean, some kind of love.

speaker: interviewer
text: After--

speaker: interviewee
text: This kind of-- this kind of life went on. And finally, we were transferred to a place called Bayerisch Gmain. Bayerisch Gmain, well, it's a tough time to spell, but I believe it's B-A-Y-E-R-I-C-H-- C-H-- C-S-H. And Gmain, the second word, is G-M-A-I-N.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is a-- this is--

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a-- this is a small town under-- almost at the Austrian border, right next to Bad Reichenhall. Bad Reichenhall was the headquarters of Hitler during in the wintertime, so there was tremendous amount of American soldiers there, where we first time started to get help from the American soldiers, where they first then they started to understand that we have there hundreds of kids, and hundreds of little kids, I mean, babies, from practically babies to seven, eight, nine-year-old. That was-- they were the big kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started to organize them and try to teach them. Part of the teaching was a horrible teaching, of trying to learn how to be quiet, how to suffer, how not to ask for drink, how not to ask to go to the bathroom, because this way, the kids won't be prepared. Our job was to prepare them to go on Aliyah.

speaker: interviewee
text: They are the one who were put on boats. They were the one who were put on ships and sent to first possible way to go to at that time Palestine. And it wasn't an easy task. And it wasn't an easy task for-- for kids with whom you spent two, three months of-- gave everything what you had. Then you had to go and cross another border and come back and bring back new kids, because these kids were gone, so you had to start with a new family. They became your family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A couple of months ago I had a visit here. I got a phone call from a-- one of my kids. He's an Israeli Merchant Marine captain. But he was one of my kids. Most of the kids, where they went to Israel to [INAUDIBLE], which is a kibbutz where most of them went. And even there, the head of the kibbutz is one of the close friends whose picture I have with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have contact with many of these children today.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yes, I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: These grown-up children.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not many of the children, but some of them I do. Some of them, I mean, very close friends. Yes, definitely I do.

speaker: interviewer
text: After Bayerisch, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We stayed basically-- I stayed in Germany basically 'til 1947 July. In 1947 June, we had the task of loading a famous ship, which was called [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And after I loaded up probably 150 to 200 of our kids and we're supposed to go on, my cousin and I decided that we are not going. And we took-- in Marseille we went to the railroad station and we saw a train which said it's leaving for Paris, so we got on the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you not go on that--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I'll be honest with you, that we were horrified they way it's going. And we heard already-- we knew that they are not allowed into Palestine, that they are going out to another camp. And-- and the idea was not-- that was also not clear-- it wasn't clear in our mind that we want-- we want to do that.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Paris, how long did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Paris, I stayed 'til 1952 May.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what?

speaker: interviewee
text: From 1947-- from 1947 to 1952, and I did all kind of work, education. 1952, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] went to Canada.

speaker: interviewer
text: What part?

speaker: interviewee
text: Montreal, Canada. Montreal, Canada, where I started to-- I mean, I worked for a company. And later on I became independent. And later on I started my own company. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank goodness I'm married. Three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your children's names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Evan, the oldest, Evan Mark. He was born in 1961. The next one after a couple of miscarriages was Laurence. And Laurence was born in 1968, and the baby, Randi, was born in 1973.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that was the second type-- the second type of change in-- especially in French Canada, where the basic idea was that I didn't believe in and today I don't believe that because of the separatist ideas and basically disliking anybody but French-Canadians, that with the Gottlieb name my children have any-- my children will have any future, although my French even today is better than my English. My children are bilingual. But I figure that their life definitely should be anywhere where there's freedom and freedom exists. And if there's anywhere freedom exists, it's the United States of America. And selfishly, I said that once I go to America, I prefer where the sun is shining, so we came to Florida.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your expectations of the United States, have they been met?

speaker: interviewee
text: More than met. I'm very, very happy, very-- I became a very proud American, who probably more American than born Americans.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Your-- your story is absolutely remarkable. Why do you come forward to talk about it today? Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a long, long time, I didn't. A long time I didn't. I was even reluctant and very, very seldom and very like in a nutshell, I was talking to my own kids. And one day, if you have enough time I tell you that I was called to my older son's school by the principal to tell me that I should be there next morning at 10 o'clock.

speaker: interviewer
text: I went there. I had no idea. My question well, what did he do? What did he do wrong? He didn't tell me, just be there. I went there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And suddenly I was ushered into the gym, where I had 300 kids sitting. And suddenly my son gets up. And I had no idea where I'm going, what I'm doing. And my son introduced me as an endangered specie. And he said, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my dad is going to talk about the Holocaust. And I was frozen. And then I realized that I owe it-- I owe it to those people who died. I owe it to the people who perished, because if I don't talk about it, there are still idiots who are telling today that the Holocaust is a hoax, the same people who are telling you the earth is flat. At least, that's my belief, including the geniuses, university professors, who tried to teach the world the Holocaust was a hoax. And if I don't stand up and if I-- we survivors don't talk, it may happen again. Maybe we can stop it. Maybe we can say it should never happen again anywhere, to any race, color, religion where a minority could be a scapegoat, because we were scapegoats.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mr. Gottlieb, after being through what you have been through, what are your feelings about God and religion?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- beyond any question that-- I mean, God has-- has a place. My question is what I'm doing today, that I think that I did, I continue to do and I will do it, to do the best possible Jewish education to my children, because that's the way my parents wanted it. That's the way I grew up. And that's what-- that's the type of education that I was given. And from there, it's up to them to-- too go on the right road.

speaker: interviewer
text: What message do you have for the world, for your children, for the great-grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: To try to do anything possible that it should never be any kind of dictatorship of any dictator on Earth, and try to work on it that we should have freedom. And to anybody who doesn't know, they should read basically what is the-- one of the most beautiful document beside of religion is the American Bill of Rights. And maybe we can stop any dictator to have scapegoats again and kill people for no reason, no rhyme or reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time, on behalf of Survivors of the Shoah of Visual History Foundation, I want to thank you for giving, as I said before, a most remarkable, most eloquent [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] interview. We have learned-- I thank you. I thank you for listening and trying to-- to make part of it that people should know that it really happened, period. Thank you.
